title: Blue native-PAGE of protein complexes in plant cells
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"8e7no","text":"Many protein complexes exist in cells, and these protein complexes are vital for cellular processes. However, much of our current analyses of protein-protein interaction mainly focus on binary interactions. Non-denaturing PAGE (polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis) provides an efficient method that enables analysis of multimeric protein complexes in vivo. In contrast to chromatography or sucrose fractionation methods, small amount of sample is required for analysis, and multiple combinations of samples can be tested simultaneously. Non-denaturing PAGE using the Coomassie G-250 dye, also known as blue native-PAGE resolves protein complexes according to their molecular weight and has been successfully used to identify protein complexes bound to membranes such as in mitochondria and chloroplasts. Recently, intracellular immune receptors in plants localizing in the cytoplasm, nucleus or plasma membrane were analyzed using the blue-native PAGE method. Here we share our method of resolving protein complexes from plant extracts or immunoprecipitated samples from plant cells on blue native-PAGE.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":347,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"1nmjd","text":"* Although this protocol used the NativePAGETM system from Thermo Fisher Scientific, similar set-ups from different suppliers, as well as setting up your own gel without SDS works just as well.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":44,"length":2}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2061n","text":"* Ensure samples are always kept on ice, and tissue samples are frozen immediately, to prevent dissociation or aggregation of protein complexes.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"75qp5","text":"* Protein extraction buffer can significantly affect protein complex stability as well as extraction. Salt concentration of buffer, type and concentration of detergent used, as well as concentration of DTT are all critical factors that determin protein exractabiligy. Therefore, optimal extraction buffer must be empirically tested for each protein of interest.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4aou3","text":"One way to check extractability of buffer is to test whether any proteins remain in the pellet after centrifugation of cell debris. Protein extracts using different buffer conditions can be loaded onto blue-native PAGE to compare which buffer condition is ideal for extracting protein complexes. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7ha0f","text":"However, not all buffer conditions ideal for extracting proteins will be ideal for preserving native states of protein complexes. The protein extraction buffer condition provided in this protocol is ideal for detecting oligomerization of NRC2, and related experimental data can be found in attachment.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fead5","text":"* Concentration of plant extracts are critical for running blue native-PAGE. If bands appear smeary, it is likely due to high concentration of protein extracts leading to aggregation. Reducing the protein extraction concentration by dilution may resolve this issue. In particular, when using protein extracts, rubisco complex (~500kDa) should be clearly visible, in addition to bands at ~720kDa, and 1,000kDa for N. benthamiana extracts.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"505qd","text":"* Accumulation of protein complexes identified in BN-PAGE must correlate with protein accumulation observed on SDS-PAGE.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fnhr0","text":"* 2 Dimensional SDS-PAGE should be performed to check that the protein bands observed on blue native-PAGE constitute of proteins that run on the same size on SDS-PAGE.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3nr1h","text":"* The relevant position of the bands observed must be consistent throughout multiple replicates. This enables measuring the approximate size of protein complex with standard curve from the markers.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"886ua","text":"This protocol was developed over time by Hee-Kyung Ahn in the Jonathan Jones group. This method has been optimized mainly for detecting the oligomers of NRC NLRs in Solacaceae, but we have tested that this protocol also applies to NLRs in Arabidopsis seedlings and rosette leaves. For other proteins, additional optimization may be needed, and we are continuing to optimize the method for other NLR proteins. We hope this protocol will be useful, and help generate new discoveries in the plant biology community and beyond. If you have any suggestions or feedback regarding this protocol, please email hee-kyung.ahn@tsl.ac.uk.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: The following cases describe sample harvest methods using trasiently-infiltrated Nicotiana benthamiana (4-5 weeks old), Arabidopsis seedlings (~2 weeks old), or Arabidopsis leaves (4-5 weeks old.)

Grind samples in 2 ml tubes using a tissue homogenizer (GenoGrinder), at 1200 rpm for 00:01:00 . Samples should be kept frozen at all times, and put sample tubes back into liquid nitrogen immediately after sample grinding is complete. Other equipments that enable rupturing frozen tissues with tungsten beads, for example, TissueLyser or other bead mills can all be used.

NativePAGE™ 3 to 12% pre-cast gel is used with XCell SureLock ™ Mini-Cell system. Although this method was optimized with pre-cast gel system from Thermo Fisher Scientific, other suppliers, as well as custom-made gels with varying gel percentages can be used for blue native-PAGE analysis as well.

After gels are run until dye fron reaches end of gel, gel cassette is pry open using cassette opening lever/gel knife. Lift open all three sides of the cassette, and carefully cut both ends of the gel (side with wells and the opposite side where the dye front is).
Open tube lids on ice, and add extraction buffer (see Materials, Buffer recipes). The amount should be 200 µL, per leaf disk , or 200 µL, for 0.01g tissue weight .
Vortex for 00:00:15 .
Incubate sample on rotator (must be kept cold) for 00:10:00 .
Centrifuge at 4°C, 13,000rpm, for 00:15:00 .

Transfer supernatant to clean new tube, centrifuge again at 4°C, 13,000 rpm for 00:05:00 . Measure protein concentration using Bradford assay, and normalize protein concentration to the sample with lowest protein concentration. However, using the same number of leaf disks during sampling, and adding same amount of extraction buffer ensures total protein concentration is similar across multiple samples.

Make Blue Native-PAGE samples from protein extracts. It is advised to make master-mix of the 4x Native sample buffer, 5% Coomassie G-250 additive, and water. For 40 µL of sample: 4x NativePAGE TM sample buffer 20 µL 5% Coomassie G-250 Additive 2 µL Distilled water 18 µL is added to make a total of 80 µL . Water is added to dilute the ion concentration of the protein extraction buffer to half strength. It is advised that the ion concentration of samples are below 50 mM. However, higher ion concentrations can be used, if all of the wells for blue native-PAGE will be loaded with buffers of similar strength. Do not boil samples, and keep the samples on ice at all times.

Make SDS-PAGE samples from the same sample protein extracts. This is to ensure same samples are used for both blue native-PAGE and SDS-PAGE. For 40 µL of sample: 3x SDS sample buffer 20 µL 1 M DTT 8 µL Distilled water 12 µL
Incubate samples at 70°C for 00:10:00 . After incubation, these samples are ready for SDS-PAGE.

Grind samples in 2 ml tubes using a tissue homogenizer (GenoGrinder), at 1200 rpm for 00:01:00 . Samples should be kept frozen at all times, and put sample tubes back into liquid nitrogen immediately after sample grinding is complete. Other equipments that enable rupturing frozen tissues with tungsten beads, for example, TissueLyser or other bead mills can all be used.
Open tube lids on ice, and add extraction buffer (see Materials). The amount should be 50 µL, for 0.01g tissue weight .
Vortex for 10 seconds. 00:00:15 .
Incubate sample on rotation (must be kept cold) for 00:10:00 .
Centrifuge at 4°C, 13,000 rpm, for 00:15:00 .

Transfer supernatant to clean new tube, centrifuge again at 4°C, 13,000 rpm for 00:05:00 . Measure protein concentration using Bradford assay, and normalize protein concentration to the sample with lowest protein concentration. However, using the same number of leaf disks during sampling, and adding same amount of extraction buffer ensures total protein concentration is similar across multiple samples.

Make Blue Native-PAGE samples from protein extracts. It is advised to make master-mix of the 4x Native sample buffer, 5% Coomassie G-250 additive, and water. For 40 µL of sample: 4x NativePAGE TM sample buffer 20 µL 5% Coomassie G-250 Additive 2 µL Distilled water 18 µL is added to make a total of 80 µL . Water is added to dilute the ion concentration of the protein extraction buffer to half strength. It is advised that the ion concentration of samples are below 50 mM. However, higher ion concentrations can be used, if all of the wells for blue native-PAGE will be loaded with buffers of similar strength. Do not boil samples, and keep the samples on ice at all times.

Make SDS-PAGE samples from the same sample protein extracts. This is to ensure same samples are used for both blue native-PAGE and SDS-PAGE. For 40 µL of sample: 3x SDS sample buffer 20 µL 1 M DTT 8 µL Distilled water 12 µL
Incubate samples at 70°C for 00:10:00 .
Open tube lids on ice, and add extraction buffer (see Materials). The amount should be 50 µL, per leaf disk , or 50 µL, for 0.01g tissue weight .
Vortex for 10 seconds. 00:00:15 .
Incubate sample on rotation (must be kept cold) for 00:10:00 .
Centrifuge at 4°C, 13,000 rpm, for 00:15:00 .

Transfer supernatant to clean new tube, centrifuge again at 4°C, 13,000 rpm for 00:05:00 . Measure protein concentration using Bradford assay, and normalize protein concentration to the sample with lowest protein concentration. However, using the same number of leaf disks during sampling, and adding same amount of extraction buffer ensures total protein concentration is similar across multiple samples.
Grind the N. benthamiana leaf samples using GenoGrinder, at 1200 rpm for 00:02:00 .
Add extraction buffer, 1.7 mL, per sample to 2 ml tubes. This is to ensure buffer does not overflow.
Vortex for 00:00:15 .
Incubate with rotation (in cold) for 00:10:00 .
Add 0.4 mL of new extraction buffer in clean tube, and transfer 1.6 mL supernatant from step 6.
Centrifuge at 4°C, 13,000 rpm, for 00:15:00 .

Take 40 µL of protein extract from each sample, and make blue native-PAGE samples. It is advised to make master-mix of the 4x Native sample buffer, 5% Coomassie G-250 additive, and water. For 40 µL of sample: 4x NativePAGE TM sample buffer 20 µL 5% Coomassie G-250 Additive 2 µL Distilled water 18 µL is added to make a total of 80 µL . Water is added to dilute the ion concentration of the protein extraction buffer to half strength. It is advised that the ion concentration of samples are below 50 mM. However, higher ion concentrations can be used, if all of the wells for blue native-PAGE will be loaded with buffers of similar strength. Do not boil samples, and keep the samples on ice at all times.

Take 40 µL of protein extract from each sample, and make SDS-PAGE samples. This is to ensure same samples are used for both blue native-PAGE and SDS-PAGE, and can also be used as input of the following immunoprecipitation experiment. For 40 µL of sample: 3x SDS sample buffer 20 µL 1 M DTT 8 µL Distilled water 12 µL
Incubate samples at 70°C for 00:10:00 .
Equilibrate beads with extraction buffer. Ideally, this step can be be done during centrifugation of protein extracts.
Centrifuge at 4°C, 13,000 rpm, for 00:05:00 .
Transfer supernatant to clean tube.

40 µL, per sample bead slurry (50% slurry for Flag M2 beads, Sigma A2220) is taken and transferred to 2 ml tube. The bead slurry is viscous, and narrow end of the tip may interferre with obtaining the correct amount of beads. To prevent this, cut 200 μl tip at the end before taking the bead slurry.
Add extraction buffer to fill the 2 ml tube, and invert the tubes several times so that beads are mixed well.
Centrifuge at 5,000 rpm for 00:01:00 , and remove remove supernatant.
Repeat steps 12.2 and 12.3 two more times.
Add 100 µL, per sample to the beads. If 120 μl bead slurry will be used for three samples, add 300 μl extraction buffer after removing final supernatant.
In a new 2 ml tube, transfer 100 μl of bead + buffer from step 12.5. The end of pipette tips should be cut off when pipetting beads to ensure accuracy.
Add protein extracts from step 9 to tubes containing beads.
Incubate on rotator (kept cold), for 02:00:00 .
Centrifuge samples at 4°C, 5,000 rpm for 00:01:00 .
Remove supernatant, add fresh wash buffer, and invert several times and make sure beads are floating.
Repeat steps 15 and 16 for 2 more times with wash buffer.
Repeat steps 15 and 16 for 2 more times with extraction buffer.

Prepare elution buffer with 3x Flag peptide. 5 mg, /ml stock concentration of 3x Flag peptides is prepared, and final concentration of 100 µg, /ml to 300 µg, /ml is used. Optimal final concentration is determined empirically.
To remove all of the buffer after the last wash, use gel loading tips or taper 200 μl tips with flat steel apparatus (e.g. back of spatula).
Add 100 µL of elution buffer from step 19 to each sample. Make sure that each sample is processed one by one, so that the beads do not stay dehydrated for too long.

Incubate the beads on shaker (kept cold) for 01:00:00 . Each apparatus used across different labs are different. Therefore, it is important to check the beads remain afloat during the elution process.
Using tapered 200 μl tip, collect supernatants. After all supernatant is taken, cut off the end of the tip to remove any beads binding to the tip, and transfer eluate to new 1.5 ml tubes.

Take 40 µL of IP eluate from each sample, and make blue native-PAGE samples. It is advised to make master-mix of the 4x Native sample buffer, 5% Coomassie G-250 additive, and water. For 40 µL of sample: 4x NativePAGE TM sample buffer 20 µL 5% Coomassie G-250 Additive 2 µL Distilled water 18 µL is added to make a total of 80 µL . Water is added to dilute the ion concentration of the protein extraction buffer to half strength. It is advised that the ion concentration of samples are below 50 mM. However, higher ion concentrations can be used, if all of the wells for blue native-PAGE will be loaded with buffers of similar strength. Do not boil samples, and keep the samples on ice at all times.

Take 40 µL of IP eluate from each sample, and make SDS-PAGE samples. This is to ensure same samples are used for both blue native-PAGE and SDS-PAGE, and can also be used as input of the following immunoprecipitation experiment. For 40 µL of sample: 3x SDS sample buffer 20 µL 1 M DTT 8 µL Distilled water 12 µL
Incubate samples at 70°C for 00:10:00 . After incubation, samples are ready for SDS-PAGE.
Grind samples to fine powder with mortar and pestle cooled in liquid nitrogen. Transfer finely ground powder into 50 ml tubes. Samples should not thaw during this process.
Add extraction buffer, 2 mL, per 1 g tissue . 2% PVPP should be added to extraction buffer for large-scale samples. PVPP should be added fresh and mixed well before mixing with grount plant tissue.
Vortex until extract is homogeneous.
Incubate with rotation (in cold) for 00:15:00 .
Centrifuge at 4°C, 5,000rpm, for 00:15:00 .
Pass supernatant through nylon mesh or microfilter, to remove any cell debris.
Centrifuge at 4°C, 13,000rpm, for 00:20:00 .
Transfer supernatant to clean tube.

Take 40 µL of protein extract from each sample, and make blue native-PAGE samples. It is advised to make master-mix of the 4x Native sample buffer, 5% Coomassie G-250 additive, and water. However, higher ion concentrations can be used, if all of the wells for blue native-PAGE will be loaded with buffers of similar strength. Incubate samples at 70°C for 00:10:00 . After incubation, these samples are ready for SDS-PAGE.
Equilibrate beads with extraction buffer. Ideally, this step can be be done during centrifugation of protein extracts.

Centrifuge at 5,000 rpm for 00:01:00 , and remove supernatant.
Repeat steps 12.2 and 12.3 two more times.

Transfer 220 μl of bead + buffer to protein extracts. The end of pipette tips should be cut off when pipetting beads to ensure accuracy.
Incubate on rotator (kept cold), for 02:00:00 .
Centrifuge samples at 4°C, 5,000 rpm for 00:01:00 .

Take 40 µL of IP eluate from each sample, and make SDS-PAGE samples. This is to ensure same samples are used for both blue native-PAGE and SDS-PAGE, and can also be used as input of the following immunoprecipitation experiment. For 40 µL of sample: 3x SDS sample buffer 20 µL 1 M DTT 8 µL Distilled water 12 µL
Incubate samples at 70°C for 00:10:00 . After incubation, sample is ready for SDS-PAGE.
Samples can be frozen in liquid nitrogen, and stored in -70°C until further use.

Fill inner chamber with cold Cathode buffer (light) after removing comb. For some proteins, it is necessary to use the Dark Cathode buffer initially before exchanging for Light Cathode Buffer. However, for resolution of Rpi-amr3 and NRC2, light cathode buffer was sufficient. Light cathode buffer is preferred when immunoblotting, as Coomassie G-250 may interfere with immunoblotting steps.
Load samples. 10-15 μl (for 10-well gels). Add 5 μl of protein standard.
Fill outer tank with cold Anode buffer.
Gel running should be performed in cold room, or large refrigerator.

Running condition: 150 V, 45 min (1/3 run into gel) 250 V, 45 min-1 h (until dye front reaches end of the gel) Stop before dye front runs through the gel. If using dark cathode buffer and subsequent immunoblotting will be performed, it is suggested to switch to light cathode buffer after the initial 45-minute run. These gels can be used for protein transfer (next section), or separate strips can be used for 2 Dimensional blue-native/SDS-PAGE (see step case below).

Assemble transfer cassette. Avoid forming air bubbles between gel and membrane. If using NativeMark™ Unstained Protein Standard, a pink marker band will appear in the middle of the gel at this stage. Poke a small hole through the gel with a pencil to label the position of this marker on the membrane. (This marker, which is ~242 kDa may not be visible after transfer.)

Transfer is run with Transblot Turbo STANDARD method (20V constant, for 00:30:00 ). Other types of semi-dry transfers can also be used, but methods must be optimized individually. For wet-type transfer, transfer for 70 V (maximum 400 mA or lower) for 01:00:00 in cold room, using CAPS buffer (10 mM CAPS, calibrated to pH 11.0 with NaOH). The running time may need to be adjusted empirically.
After transfer is over, incubate the transferred membrane in 8% acetic acid for 00:15:00 .
Rinse membrane with clean water two times briefly.
Place membrane on filter paper and let dry. Up to 1 h in the fume hood, or overnight on bench.
After membrane is completely dry, re-activate membrane with ethanol.
As membrane is re-activated, Coomassie dye will wash out and markers will show up at this stage. It is crucial to label all visible markers with pencil, as they will gradually disappear.

For some proteins, the epitope may be buried within the protein complex making it difficult to detect with antibodies. To prevent this, incubate re-activated membrane with SDS running buffer (with up to 10 mM DTT) after re-activation 1 h to overnight. Wash thoroughly with TBS-T, 5 min 3 times.

After membrane is re-activated and Coomassie dye is significantly washed away, decant the ethanol, and immediately add TBS-T. Wash membrane with TBS-T, 3 times for 2 min each. When TBS-T is first added, re-activated membrane will tend to float, and as ethanol dries up, membranes may form blue patches. This can be prevented by adding excess amount of TBS-to submerge the membrane in buffer.
Add 5% skimmed milk in TBS-T to membrane, and block for 00:30:00 .
Decant blocking solution, and add antibody in 5% skimmed milk in TBS-T for 1 h to overnight.

If using HRP conjugated antibodies, membranes can be washed with TBS-T 3 times (5 min each) and will be ready for detecting chemiluminescent signal. If using non-conjugated antibodies, wash with TBS-T 3 times (5 min each), incubate with secondary antibody (in 5% skimmed milk in TBS-T), then repeat washing step before preparing for chemiluminescent signal detection.
Cut a strip of blue-native PAGE gel after electrophoresis is over, and carefully insert into 15 ml or 50 ml tube using gel holder or blunt/membrane forcep.
Add 3X SDS sample buffer with 10 mM DTT into the tube, for 00:15:00 .
Rinse with water (at least three times). DTT and other reducing agents interfere with gel polymerization. Therefore, rinse thoroughly to remove excess DTT.

Prepare resolution gel of ideal percentage (for resolving Rpi-amr3 and NRC2, we used 8% gel). Alternatively, you can also use pre-cast gels from multiple suppliers that provide large wells in the gels to insert the gel strips. Resolution gel recipe for 8% gel: A B 1 Water 4.6 mL 2 1.5 M Tris (pH 8.8) 2.5 mL 3 30% Acrylamide 2.7 mL 4 10% SDS 0.1 mL 5 10% APS 0.1 mL 6 TEMED 0.006 mL 7 Total volume 10 mL

Insert the denatured gel strip onto the prepared resolution gel. Add stacking gel buffer to stack gel strip on top of the resolution gel. Ensure no air bubbles are stuck in between the gel strip and the resolution gel. Stacking gel recipe: A B 1 Water 1.4 mL 2 1.0 M Tris (pH 6.8) 0.25 mL 3 30% Acrylamide 0.33 mL 4 10% SDS 0.02 mL 5 10% APS 0.02 mL 6 TEMED 0.001 mL 7 Total 2 mL
Run with identical conditions for SDS-PAGE.
